wrong_fixes.note: Adds the None guard from the usual remedy but leaves the default as a literal list, so the guard never fires.
Hands back a copy, but the shared default is still the object being appended to on every call.
Tightens the trim boundary, which has nothing to do with the history surviving between calls.
Swaps in the copy() method, which is still a one-level copy: the nested dict is the same object.
Copies the incoming override rather than the nested default it is about to write into.
Copies the outer dict a second time; the nested value inside it is still shared with the defaults.
Moves the default into get(), but the truthiness test below still discards an explicit zero.
Adds a membership check, which is true here; the falsy test that actually discards the zero is untouched.
Uses or to supply the default, which is the same falsy test written a different way.
Stops converting the raw identifier, which changes what is compared but not how it is compared.
Guards against a missing identifier; the identity comparison underneath is unchanged.
Reaches the field through get(), which returns the same object the identity test was already failing on.
Reads the other half of the pair off the winner, but the winner was still chosen alphabetically.
Sorts the keys directly, which is the same alphabetical ordering with the counts thrown away.
Swaps sorted() for max() but keeps ranking by the endpoint name rather than the hit count.
Compares the printed forms, which differ for exactly the reason the numbers do.
Starts the accumulator as an integer; the first addition makes it a float again and the drift is identical.
Introduces abs() but keeps an exact comparison, so any drift at all still reports a mismatch.
Converts both sides to tuples, which builds two new objects that are still not the same object.
Adds a None guard in front of an identity test that was never going to succeed.
Normalises the target into a list, producing yet another distinct object for the identity test to miss.
Copies on the way out, long after the module-level dictionary has already been overwritten in place.
Copies the overrides, which were never the shared object; the defaults are still updated in place.
Copies the caller's first payload, which does not stop the defaults being rewritten by the update.
Narrows the stride to match the shortened window, so the pages no longer overlap but each is still short.
Widens the page size to make up the missing row, which shifts the window instead of restoring it.
Clamps the end against the length of the list, which was never the reason a row was missing.
Swaps remove() for pop() with an explicit index, which shortens the list being walked in exactly the same way.
Walks the list backwards, which survives the deletion but hands the rows back in the reverse order.
Copies each row on the way into the result, which does nothing about the list being shortened underneath the walk.
Iterates keys() explicitly, which is the same live view over the dictionary being resized.
Swaps del for pop(), which removes the entry and changes the size just the same.
Loosens the floor comparison, which changes which entries are dropped but not that dropping them breaks the walk.
Uses the other integer conversion, which truncates the score in precisely the same way.
Rounds the score before handing it over, which still delivers a whole number to a whole-number conversion.
Switches to a float conversion but asks for no decimal places, so the half point is rounded away instead of truncated.
Adds the base price to the key, which is the same for both tiers; the multiplier is still missing.
Keys on the whole item, which is also identical across the two tiers being priced.
Writes with setdefault so an existing entry is never overwritten, which is what was already happening.
Suppresses decoding errors, but the mismatched codec never raised one: it silently produced the wrong text.
Adds a replacement policy on the writing side; the reader is still using a different codec.
Reads back as ASCII with replacement, which is a third codec rather than the one that wrote the bytes.
Names the lambda before storing it, which changes nothing about when the loop variable is read.
Binds a default argument, which is the right shape, but binds the first offset to every step.
Closes over an index instead of the value, which is late-bound in exactly the same way.
Adds the None guard from the usual remedy while leaving the default as a literal dict, so the guard is dead code.
Returns a copy, which leaves the caller with fresh data but keeps writing into the one shared default.
Uses setdefault so an existing span is never overwritten, which was never how the earlier request's span got in.
Measures the ASCII-only encoding, which drops the very character that made the label overrun.
Writes ASCII bytes instead, which shortens the payload but still measures the unencoded string.
Trims the label before packing it, which changes nothing for a label that has no surrounding whitespace.
Flips the subtraction, which turns the sign around without moving the instant that was mislabelled.
Divides instead of flooring, which changes nothing for a gap that is a whole number of minutes.
Relabels the deadline as well, which is already in UTC, so only the start remains shifted.
